title: Little Caesar
description: Little Caesar — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "Little Caesar" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: Dedication
memes.description: For

memes.label: Epigraph
memes.description: "> The first law of every being, is to preserve itself and live. You sow hemlock, and expect to see ears of corn ripen

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Ambition
memes.description: The driving force of Rico Bandello's character and the novel's central theme — the desire for power, status, and recognition that propels the gangster's rise and guarantees his fall.

memes.label: Antonio Passalacqua
memes.description: A minor Chicago underworld figure whose name appears in legal or police contexts — one of the many names that populate the novel's background of crime and enforcement.

memes.label: Arnold Worch
memes.description: A North Side underworld figure mentioned in a newspaper item as part of the broader criminal ecosystem that surrounds the novel's central gang conflict.

memes.label: Arrigo
memes.description: An associate who provides Rico Bandello with an army shirt during his escape from Chicago — a minor but humanizing figure in the fugitive's support network.

memes.label: "Banquet for Rico"
memes.description: "The celebratory banquet thrown by Sam Vettori's gang in honor of Rico Bandello — a lavish, chaotic set piece at the novel's midpoint that marks the peak of Rico's power, public recognition, and the beginning of his overreach."

memes.label: Bat Carillo
memes.description: A former third-rate light-heavyweight boxer turned gang enforcer, once the leader of a small-time crew absorbed into Sam Vettori's operation, now reduced to the role of bouncer at the Palermo Club.

memes.label: Betrayal
memes.description: A central organizing principle of the criminal world in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — the constant threat of informants, double-crosses, and treacherous alliances that drives the novel's plot and shapes every character relationship.

memes.label: Big Boy
memes.description: A powerful crime boss and political fixer in Chicago's underworld who controls the city's criminal operations from above, issuing orders to Sam Vettori's gang and dictating the terms under which they may operate.

memes.label: Bill Hackett
memes.description: A minor Chicago gangster who dislikes Rico Bandello — a representative of the internal rivalries and personal animosities that fracture the city's underworld.

memes.label: Blackie Avezzano
memes.description: The manager of Sam Vettori's garage, a small, bowlegged, dark-complexioned Italian who handles money and logistics for the gang.

memes.label: Blondy Belle
memes.description: The most prominent woman in Chicago's Little Italy underworld — formerly Little Arnie's companion, later Rico Bandello's consort during his rise to power.

memes.label: Blue Island
memes.description: A Chicago suburb mentioned in connection with Rico Bandello's escape from the city — one of the way stations on his flight to Hammond and a marker of the geographic spread of the novel's criminal geography.

memes.label: Blue Jay
memes.description: A minor underworld figure mentioned in the novel's gangster ecosystem — one of the many street names and nicknames that populate Burnett's Chicago.

memes.label: Bugs Liska
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: "Casa Alvarado"
memes.description: "An apartment building in Chicago targeted for a major holdup by Rico Bandello's gang — the site of a pivotal robbery that marks the transition from Vettori's old-school caution to Rico's aggressive criminal ambition."

memes.label: "Casa Alvarado Robbery"
memes.description: "The robbery of the Casa Alvarado apartments in Chicago, planned and executed by Rico Bandello's crew — a major financial score that bankrolls Rico's rise and whose aftermath exposes the greed eroding gang loyalty from within."

memes.label: Chicago Police Department
memes.description: The law enforcement institution that pursues the novel's gangster protagonists — a pervasive background presence whose investigations, arrests, and raids constitute the counter-force to organized crime's operations.

memes.label: Chicago Red
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: Chicago Tribune
memes.description: The major Chicago newspaper whose crime coverage shapes public perception of the gangster world — the media institution that both creates the celebrity Rico Bandello craves and provides the visibility that ultimately helps destroy him.

memes.label: Class
memes.description: The unspoken hierarchy that shapes every character's ambitions and limitations in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — the gangster's true enemy is not the police but the social order that excludes him.

memes.label: Corruption
memes.description: The institutional lubricant of organized crime in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — political and police corruption enables the gangster economy and determines who rises and who falls.

memes.label: "Courriere Holdup"
memes.description: "The pivotal robbery in Little Caesar that goes wrong, catalyzing Rico Bandello's hostile takeover of Sam Vettori's gang and setting the novel's central conflict — ambition versus loyalty — in motion."

memes.label: Courtney
memes.description: A Chicago police officer whose killing during the Courriere holdup becomes the pivotal event driving the novel's second-half manhunt and Rico Bandello's eventual downfall.

memes.label: Crabby Louis
memes.description: A regular gambler at Sansotta's card room in Hammond — one of the small-time underworld figures whose days revolve around cards, pool tables, and the ritual call of "Well, Crabby Louis, it's your shot."

memes.label: Detroit
memes.description: An industrial city in Michigan that serves as an outlying territory in the novel's gangster geography — a source of outside gunmen and a destination for Chicago gangsters seeking to expand their operations.

memes.label: DeVoss
memes.description: The manager of The Bronze Peacock, an upscale Chicago nightclub — a square businessman caught between the legitimate entertainment world and the gangsters who frequent his establishment.

memes.label: Dutch Schaefer
memes.description: A minor underworld figure in Chicago's gangster ecosystem — one of the non-Italian criminals who operated at the margins of the city's ethnically organized crime world.

memes.label: Escape and Flight
memes.description: The gangster's final resort in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — when power fails and allies betray, flight is the only option, and it is never enough.

memes.label: Ethnicity and Crime
memes.description: The complex relationship between ethnic identity and organized crime in 1920s Chicago — the immigrant social structures, prejudices, and aspirations that shaped the gangster world depicted in Little Caesar.

memes.label: Family
memes.description: The absent institution in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — the gang replaces the family as the primary social unit, but it offers none of the family's protections.

memes.label: Fate and Inevitability
memes.description: The governing logic of gangster life in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — every rise contains its fall, and the gangster's trajectory is predetermined by forces he cannot control.

memes.label: Flaherty
memes.description: A plainclothes detective with the Chicago police force, investigating the Vettori gang's criminal activities throughout the novel.

memes.label: Francis Wood
memes.description: The owner of the Casa Alvarado nightclub, a legitimate businessman whose establishment becomes the target of Sam Vettori's gang's most ambitious robbery.

memes.label: Friendship
memes.description: The doomed personal bond at the heart of W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — the friendship between Rico Bandello and Joe Massara is the novel's emotional core and its central tragedy.

memes.label: Gang Hierarchy
memes.description: The rigid social structure of the criminal underworld depicted in Little Caesar — a ladder from street-level stickup men through crew leaders and gang bosses to the shadowy "fixers" who control the system from above.

memes.label: "Gangster Code"
memes.description: "The unwritten code of conduct governing the Chicago underworld in Little Caesar — a Darwinian system where softness is the only unforgivable sin, loyalty is fear-based, and power belongs to whoever is ruthless enough to seize it."

memes.label: Gold Coast
memes.description: Chicago's wealthy lakefront neighborhood — a symbol of the legitimate prosperity and social respectability that exists in stark contrast to the gangster world of the novel's main characters, and the object of Rico Bandello's envious fascination.

memes.label: Gun Violence
memes.description: The primary instrument of power and death in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — the gun is both tool and symbol, the object that defines the gangster's relationship to the world.

memes.label: Hammond
memes.description: An industrial city in Indiana, just across the state line from Chicago, that serves as the novel's primary "outside" territory — a refuge for fugitives, a base for smaller gangs, and the setting for the novel's climactic final sequence.

memes.label: Honor and Reputation
memes.description: The currency of status and respect in the criminal underworld of W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — a gangster's name is his most valuable asset and his most fragile possession.

memes.label: Identity and Self
memes.description: Rico Bandello's construction and ultimate loss of self in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — the gangster's identity is both his greatest creation and his most fragile possession.

memes.label: Isolation
memes.description: The psychological condition of the gangster at the top in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — power separates Rico Bandello from every human connection until he faces his downfall absolutely alone.

memes.label: Italian-American Identity
memes.description: The ethnic foundation of the criminal world in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — the novel's gangsters are products of Italian immigrant communities whose marginalization shapes their ambitions and methods.

memes.label: Jew Mike
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: Jim Henderson
memes.description: A minor figure referenced in the extended background of 1920s Chicago — part of the city's institutional landscape of law, journalism, or politics that frames the gangster narrative.

memes.label: Joe Brody
memes.description: A minor Chicago underworld figure — one of the many names populating the extended criminal network that surrounds the novel's central cast.

memes.label: "Joe Massara"
memes.description: "A gang member in Sam Vettori's outfit who wants to leave the criminal life to become a professional dancer with his girlfriend Olga. His defection creates the central conflict with Rico Bandello."

memes.label: Joe Massara's Arrest
memes.description: The arrest of dancer and former gang insider Joe Massara following the Casa Alvarado robbery, a pivotal event that forces Joe to choose between loyalty to the gang and his own survival — and sets in motion Rico's escalating campaign to control his former partner.

memes.label: Joseph Pavlovsky
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: Justice
memes.description: The absent ideal in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — in a world where the police are corrupt, the law is impotent, and gangsters enforce their own rules, justice is whatever the powerful decide it is.

memes.label: Kid Bean
memes.description: A member of Sam Vettori's gang, present at the banquet for Rico and later killed during Rico's consolidation of power — one of the gang members purged as the organization transforms under new leadership.

memes.label: Kiketown
memes.description: A Chicago neighborhood referenced in the novel's geographic lexicon — one of the ethnic enclaves that defined the city's social geography in the early twentieth century and marked the boundaries between different gangs' territories.

memes.label: Killer Pepi
memes.description: A member of Sam Vettori's gang — known for his piercing shrill whistle and his shift in loyalty from Vettori to Rico Bandello as the gang's power structure realigns.

memes.label: "Killing of Tony Passa"
memes.description: "The murder of Antonio 'Tony' Passalacqua, a young Vettori gang member found dead near St. Dominick's Cathedral — a gangland killing that deepens the atmosphere of paranoia and betrayal in Little Caesar's underworld."

memes.label: Kips Berger
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: Limpy John
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: "Little Arnie's Gang"
memes.description: "A rival Chicago criminal organization controlling the North Side gambling rackets — the established power that Rico Bandello studies, undermines, and ultimately absorbs in his ascent to underworld dominance."

memes.label: Loyalty
memes.description: The binding social force of the gangster world — and the central value that Little Caesar systematically exposes as contingent, transactional, and ultimately hollow.

memes.label: Madison Street
memes.description: One of Chicago's defining east-west thoroughfares — the baseline from which the city's street numbering system originates, and a major commercial corridor that marks the boundary between Chicago's North and South Sides.

memes.label: Masculinity
memes.description: The code of masculine behavior that governs every interaction in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — toughness is both survival strategy and performance, and any sign of softness is fatal.

memes.label: Mickey Monroe
memes.description: A minor associate in the Chicago underworld — one of the names that flickers through the novel's extended cast of gangsters and their affiliates.

memes.label: Monroe Gang
memes.description: A minor Chicago criminal crew operating in the city's competitive gangster landscape — one of the smaller organizations that exist in the shadow of larger operations like Sam Vettori's and the Big Boy's network.

memes.label: Pete Montana's North Side Syndicate
memes.description: The dominant organized crime syndicate controlling Chicago's North Side, led by Pete Montana (Pietro Fontano) with lieutenant Ritz Colonna. A vast narcotics and vice empire — the established old guard that Rico Bandello's rising Vettori gang challenges.

memes.label: New Year's Eve at Sansotta's
memes.description: The New Year's Eve celebration at Sansotta's cabaret in Hammond — Rico Bandello's last night of relative freedom, culminating in the confrontation with political boss Paolo that gets Rico expelled from his final refuge.

memes.label: Newspaper and Publicity
memes.description: The double-edged sword of gangster life in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — newspaper coverage confers status but also attracts police attention, making the gangster's hunger for fame a fatal appetite.

memes.label: Olga Stassoff
memes.description: A dancer at DeVoss's Bronze Peacock nightclub and Joe Massara's girlfriend, drawn into the conflict between Joe's legitimate ambitions and Rico's criminal demands.

memes.label: Omertà
memes.description: The Sicilian code of silence — the unwritten law forbidding cooperation with authorities that underlies the gangster's moral universe in Little Caesar, and the cultural tradition that Rico Bandello both invokes and ultimately destroys.

memes.label: Otero
memes.description: A Greek gunman in Sam Vettori's Chicago gang, known as "The Greek," who participated in the Courriere holdup and served as one of Vettori's trusted specialists.

memes.label: "Palermo Club"
memes.description: "A nightclub and cabaret on Chicago's North Side where Joe Massara and Olga dance, and a frequent meeting place for Rico Bandello and his gang — the social nexus where the criminal and entertainment worlds of Little Caesar intersect."

memes.label: Pete Montana
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: Pippy Coke
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: Prohibition
memes.description: The nationwide constitutional ban on alcohol production and sale (1920–1933) that created the economic conditions for organized crime's explosive growth — the invisible engine driving every character and conflict in Little Caesar.

memes.label: Red Gus
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: Rico's War Against Little Arnie
memes.description: Rico Bandello's systematic destruction of Little Arnie's North Side gambling empire — a territorial conquest that establishes Rico as the dominant gangster in Chicago's neutral zone.

memes.label: "Rico Bandello"
memes.description: "Protagonist of W.R. Burnett's 1929 gangster novel Little Caesar — a ruthless, ambitious gangster who rises from small-time hood to crime boss in Chicago."

memes.label: Rico's Takeover of the Vettori Gang
memes.description: The internal coup in which Rico Bandello seizes control of Sam Vettori's gang following the Courriere holdup, marking the transition from old-guard criminal leadership to a new, more ruthless order.

memes.label: "Rico's Final Stand"
memes.description: "The deliberately anti-climactic death of Rico Bandello on a Hammond, Indiana street — shot by a patrolman who never learns his name, stripping the gangster myth of all romance in the novel's definitive moral statement."

memes.label: Rico's Flight from Chicago
memes.description: Rico Bandello's desperate escape from Chicago after Joe Massara's betrayal — the beginning of the end for the gangster who once ruled the North Side.

memes.label: Ritz Colonna
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: Sam Vettori
memes.description: The leader of the Vettori gang — a big, fat, declining kingpin whose grip on Chicago's Little Italy underworld slips as Rico Bandello rises to supplant him.

memes.label: Sansotta
memes.description: A small-time gang leader and cabaret owner in Hammond, Indiana who provides refuge to Rico Bandello during his final days as a fugitive — and ultimately ejects him after Rico's recklessness threatens his operation.

memes.label: Scabby
memes.description: A police informer secretly working for Sam Vettori's gang, whose rat-like appearance and treacherous nature make him one of the novel's most morally repulsive figures.

memes.label: Sicily Pete
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: Snitching
memes.description: The act of informing to authorities — the cardinal sin in the gangster's moral code, the betrayal that destroys organizations, and the specific charge Rico Bandello levels against Joe Massara as the novel's central conflict crystallizes.

memes.label: Speakeasy Culture
memes.description: The hidden drinking establishments of Prohibition-era Chicago that served as both gangster infrastructure and social gathering places in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar.

memes.label: Spike Rieger
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: Squint Maschke
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: St. Dominick's Cathedral
memes.description: A Catholic church in Chicago referenced in the novel as part of the city's ethnic and religious landscape — one of the institutional anchors of the Italian-American community from which the novel's gangsters emerge.

memes.label: State Street
memes.description: One of Chicago's major commercial thoroughfares, referenced in the novel's urban geography — the "great street" of retail, theaters, and traffic that forms the legitimate city's backbone, in constant tension with the gangster world that operates in its shadows.

memes.label: Steve Gollancz
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: Stickup
memes.description: The armed robbery — the primary operational method of Sam Vettori's gang and the defining activity that shapes the novel's action, characters, and ultimate tragedy.

memes.label: Surveillance
memes.description: The constant state of being watched — by police, informants, and rivals — that defines the psychological atmosphere of W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar.

memes.label: "Territory"
memes.description: "The spatial logic of underworld power in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — a system where geographic boundaries define gang hierarchy, neutral zones buffer rival factions, and the phrase 'out of your territory' is a challenge to authority as much as a statement of fact."

memes.label: The Beer Racket
memes.description: The illegal brewing, distribution, and sale of beer during Prohibition — the most lucrative and fiercely contested sector of organized crime in 1920s Chicago, and the economic foundation upon which gangs like Sam Vettori's are built.

memes.label: "The Rackets"
memes.description: "The organized criminal enterprises — gambling, bootlegging, robbery, and protection — that form the economic engine of Chicago's underworld in Little Caesar, driving every character's choices in Burnett's unsentimental portrait of crime as business."

memes.label: Toledo
memes.description: An Ohio industrial city mentioned in the novel's gangster geography — one of the Midwest cities in the orbit of Chicago's organized crime network, associated with fugitive hideouts and satellite criminal operations.

memes.label: Tom MacIlvaine
memes.description: A minor figure in the extended criminal-legal ecosystem of 1920s Chicago — potentially a police officer, journalist, or legal figure referenced in the novel's background.

memes.label: Tony Passa
memes.description: A young, eager member of Sam Vettori's gang whose murder early in the novel sets off a chain of investigations and reprisals that destabilize the gang's operations.

memes.label: Killing of Tony Passa
memes.description: The murder of young gang member Tony Passa — one of the novel's first violent casualties within Sam Vettori's inner circle, an event that brings police scrutiny and accelerates the gang's internal disintegration.

memes.label: Twenty-Second Street
memes.description: A Chicago street referenced in the novel's urban geography — one of the many street names that map the city's gangster geography with documentary precision.

memes.label: "Vettori Gang"
memes.description: "The Chicago criminal organization led by Sam Vettori at the start of Little Caesar — later commandeered by Rico Bandello, who transforms it from a cautious neighborhood crew into an aggressive North Side power before its ultimate collapse."

memes.label: Violence
memes.description: The foundational tool of power and the constant atmospheric condition of gangster life in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — violence is both career strategy and existential threat.

memes.label: Wealth
memes.description: The visible marker of criminal success and the object of Rico Bandello's deepest yearning in W.R. Burnett's Little Caesar — money is power made tangible.

memes.label: Willoughby
memes.description: A millionaire who frequents The Bronze Peacock nightclub — a figure from Chicago's legitimate elite whose presence highlights the porous boundary between high society and the gangster underworld.

memes.label: Young Chiggi
memes.description: A character in W.R. Burnett's novel Little Caesar.

memes.label: Youngstown
memes.description: An industrial city in Ohio, mentioned as Rico Bandello's birthplace — a working-class steel town whose tough, immigrant character shaped the novel's protagonist and serves as an origin story in miniature.

memes.label: Youngstown Louis
memes.description: An alias of Luigi De Angelo, a minor underworld figure operating in Sansotta's card room in Hammond — a small-time gambler whose nickname links him to Rico Bandello's hometown.
